paragraphs: Six positions on how I work. These are commitments — what I aim for, not what I always pull off.

pledges.title: Culture is king
pledges.text: Inclusive teams produce better work than uniform ones. The ideas a monoculture misses only show up in rooms where it's safe to disagree. A leader's job is building that room.

pledges.title: Framing is first
pledges.text: I push on definitions before estimates. Most of the engineering disasters I've watched were technically clean, written well, shipped on time, but were answering the wrong question. Time spent on the framing is costs less than misdirected building, builds trust and team cohesion.

pledges.title: Documentation and clear decisions
pledges.text: Keeping a log of architectural decisions is now more than just good practice for future engineers. It's core to the agentic approach. This site has thirty-one so far, including the ones that document deviations and reversals. The point is being able to argue with a past decision when context shifts and keep the LLMs in the loop on why.

pledges.title: Right-aligned development
pledges.text: Before picking up new work, scan the board right-to-left. Looking for: someone who needs a review, someone stuck on something I have context on, someone working in an area I'm about to touch. Usually one of those is a cheaper unblock than starting new work. I've been calling it right-aligned development (RAD).

pledges.title: Honesty over polish
pledges.text: I'd rather a CV bullet or an ADR read truthfully than impressively. The JD matcher on this site is tuned to flag my own gaps as honest gaps rather than stretch them into matches.

pledges.title: No absolutism in technological choices
pledges.text: My default criteria for tech choices: SOLID, maintainability, and agentic readiness — tools and structures an LLM can reason about. I'll break any of them deliberately when the product needs a different balance, provided I write down why. For example, ADR-0001 picks Next.js over the LLM design's recommended Astro.
